methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search was attempted first, including reduced limit=1 retries, but returned Insufficient credits. No Firecrawl-discovered source was used without independent official-source verification.
Found AI at Duke student guidance, teaching hub, tools page, and department/course pages; department/course pages were rejected for limited scope.
Found Duke Community Standard academic dishonesty page and AI at Duke student guidance.
Found CTL assignment-design guidance and AI Tools and Resources page; event and training pages rejected.
Found CTL teaching and syllabus-policy pages and AI at Duke teaching resources.
Found Duke Community Standard page and course-level sources; course-specific pages rejected.
Found official OIT and AI at Duke tool pages; AI Tools and Resources used for claim support.
Search returned Duke Today news and official AI/data pages; news result rejected and privacy/security claim sourced from AI Tools and myResearchPath pages.
Confirmed official Duke AI, CTL, Community Standard, and myResearchPath guidance pages; third-party/reddit result rejected.

artifacts.summary: Primary Firecrawl discovery was blocked by insufficient credits. Fallback discovery found official Duke guidance for student AI use, academic integrity, approved tools, teaching/syllabus policy, assignment design, and research use. Rejected results were news/event/training/course/department-only or third-party pages.

targets.sourceTitle: Artificial Intelligence Policies: Guidelines and Considerations

stopConditions: Stop after official Duke pages cover student AI use, academic integrity, approved tools, teaching and assessment guidance, privacy/security, and research guidance.
Do not use rejected, login-required, news-only, event-only, department-only, course-only, vendor, third-party, or generic pages for approved claims.
Do not mark claims human_reviewed or publish canonical claims.

artifacts.claimText: Duke student-facing AI guidance says whether a student may use AI in coursework depends on instructor permission; unauthorized generative AI use is considered academic misconduct under the Duke Community Standard.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Student AI use depends on instructor permission; unauthorized generative AI use is academic misconduct.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The answer is: it depends! Per the Duke Community Standard, the unauthorized use of generative AI is considered academic misconduct. However, your instructor may permit or at times ask you to use generative AI in certain circumstances and assignments.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Duke student AI use depends on instructor permission and the Duke Community Standard.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Section: Can I use AI on my Coursework?

artifacts.claimText: Duke Community Standard academic-dishonesty guidance includes unauthorized use of artificial intelligence software among examples of cheating-related conduct.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Unauthorized AI software use is listed in Duke academic-dishonesty guidance.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Cheating is the act of wrongfully using or attempting to use unauthorized materials, information, study aids, or the ideas or work of another. It includes, but is not limited to: using, consulting, and/or maintaining unauthorized shared resources including, but not limited to, test banks, solutions materials and/or unauthorized use of artificial intelligence (AI) software
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Duke academic-dishonesty guidance explicitly includes unauthorized AI software use.

artifacts.claimText: Duke AI tool guidance describes ChatGPT as available to Duke University faculty, staff, and students, with sensitive-data use excluding PHI and governed by institutional agreement.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Duke ChatGPT guidance allows sensitive data excluding PHI under institutional agreement.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: ChatGPT is designed for a broad audience and can be used for general chatbot tasks to streamline your workflow. This includes answering questions, writing, editing, and synthesizing data and ideas. Duke University faculty, staff, and students. Prepaid licenses are provided at no cost to all undergraduate students, as well as faculty, staff, and graduate students in participating units. All other Duke users can purchase a license at a significantly discounted rate. Sensitive (no PHI), (governed by institutional agreement).
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Duke labels ChatGPT for sensitive data without PHI under an institutional agreement.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Artificial Intelligence Policies: Guidelines and Considerations
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Under the Duke Community Standard, unauthorized use of generative AI is treated as cheating. This means you have the discretion to define how, if, and when generative AI may be used in your courses. All instructors should update their syllabi to include clear guidance on the use of generative AI in their courses.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Duke CTL advises clear syllabus guidance for generative AI use.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Sections: Last modified note and Draft your own AI policy

artifacts.normalizedValue: AI assignments should have course and assignment-specific AI policies aligned with learning goals.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Note, it is critical to develop AI policies for your course along with policies for specific AI assignments. In the development of AI assignments, the primary consideration is whether the use of AI will help your students achieve the learning goals of the course.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: There are ethical issues to using AI beyond questions of plagiarism, copyright and academic integrity that should be considered. First, to minimize threats to the privacy of your students and yourself, personal information should not be shared.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Using Generative AI (Artificial Intelligence) tools in research
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Do document and publish your decision-making alongside your research. Don’ts Don’t cite information found or summarized by a chatbot that you haven’t authenticated. Don’t use AI tools that collect, store, or train on user data. Check a tool’s privacy policy; if you’re not sure, contact the tool’s creator.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Duke research guidance emphasizes documentation, authentication, and privacy checks.

artifacts.title: Duke University staged AI policy crawl draft
artifacts.summary: Staged one-university crawl for Duke University, QS 2026 rank 62, United States, source language en. Official Duke sources support candidate claims about student AI use, academic integrity, approved AI tools, data/security limits, teaching and syllabus guidance, assignment design, privacy, and research guidance. All claims remain staged, agent_reviewed, and non-canonical. Firecrawl primary discovery was attempted but blocked by insufficient credits.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
This staged run does not publish canonical claims and has not received human review.
Firecrawl primary discovery was blocked by insufficient credits; fallback discovery used web search and direct official-source fetches.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
